prompt:
So our feature-flag service keeps everything in one big dict behind one big lock, and last week an audit export held that lock for nine seconds while every writer piled up behind it. I've been reading about how real databases do this and I want the same shape: multi-version concurrency control, so readers never block anything and writers only fight when they actually collide. Build it as a single stdlib-only module, mvcc.py. No threads needed in the module itself — the calling layer serializes operations; what I need is the versioning logic.

MVCCStore() is the store. begin() returns a transaction whose reads come from a snapshot: the committed state as of begin(), overlaid with the transaction's own puts/deletes (get returns None for missing or deleted). Other transactions' commits after my begin() must be invisible to me. put/delete buffer into a write set; nothing is visible to anyone else until commit().

commit() is first-committer-wins: if any key I wrote (puts AND deletes count) was committed by someone else after I began — same value or not — raise WriteConflict, roll my transaction back so none of my writes apply, and close it. The error message should name a conflicting key so people can log something useful. Reads never conflict: this is snapshot isolation, not serializable, and I'm fine with write skew. A read-only commit always succeeds. abort() throws the write set away. Once a transaction is committed, aborted, or conflicted, every further operation on it (get/put/delete/commit/abort) raises TxnClosed.

Because snapshots pin old data, the store accumulates version records — that's the part I care about getting right. db.versions(key) reports how many records a key currently holds, tombstones included (0 for unknown keys). db.vacuum() collects garbage: drop a record when no active snapshot resolves the key to it and it is not the key's newest committed record; a tombstone that is the newest record gets dropped too once every older record of its key is gone, so fully-dead keys vanish. vacuum() returns how many records it removed, and it must never change what any live snapshot reads.

Also give me db.read(key) — latest committed value, None if absent/deleted — for quick probes.

test_mvcc.py is the acceptance suite; run python3 test_mvcc.py and don't edit it.
